FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes 10,588 participants from U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; mixed-effects logistic regression at 2-year follow-up (age 12)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: three separate analyses testing associations of smartphone ownership, age of first acquisition, and recent acquisition with health outcomes

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone-specific IV (smartphone ownership, yes/no; age of first smartphone acquisition in years) paired with individual-trait health outcomes (depression diagnosis via K-SADS-5, obesity via BMI/CDC definition, insufficient sleep <9 hours). Main analysis reports OR 1.31 (95% CI 1.05-1.63) for smartphone ownership and depression; OR 1.40 (95% CI 1.20-1.63) for obesity; OR 1.62 (95% CI 1.46-1.79) for insufficient sleep.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: logistic regression ORs with 95% CIs for all health outcomes (depression, obesity, insufficient sleep) across three analyses; supplementary tables S3-11 contain additional models with continuous outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported: OR 1.31, 1.40, 1.62 (Analysis 1 at age 12); OR 1.09, 1.08 per year earlier acquisition (Analysis 2); OR 1.57, 1.50 for recent acquisition (Analysis 3). All with 95% CIs.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is mixed (cross-sectional and longitudinal across three analyses). Smartphone is IV in all analyses. Carve-out does not apply: FT-EC7 requires all three conditions (longitudinal design, SM DV-only in every analysis, no SM-as-IV anywhere). Here SM is IV throughout, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong evidence of smartphone ownership→health outcomes associations. Three separate analyses demonstrate consistent adverse effects on depression, obesity, and sleep.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes 11,875 ABCD Study participants; baseline ages 9-10 years; longitudinal data from ages 10, 11, 12 years across 3 assessment waves

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: confirmatory factor analysis extracting neurodevelopmental, externalizing, internalizing, detachment, somatoform dimensions; linear mixed models testing longitudinal associations; mediation models testing environmental and lifestyle factors as mediators

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use (composite including social media, social engagement, passive watching, active gaming per methods page 3) appears only as mediator/covariate in mediation models. Primary analysis is neurodevelopmental spectrum (IV at age 10) → psychiatric dimensions (DV at ages 11, 12), with screen use and other environmental/lifestyle factors as mediators. Per criteria §2.4 binding rule: 'SM as covariate/mediator/moderator-only fails IC2.' Screen use has no direct coefficient as IV or DV; no phone/SM-specific per-modality estimate found in main or supplementary results. Composite does not qualify as it bundles gaming, social media, passive watching, and active engagement without separate breakout.

notes: Paper examines neurodevelopmental spectrum→psychiatric outcomes; sleep problems emerge as strongest mediator (12-29% of associations). However, screen use does not qualify as a primary SM/phone variable in IC2 because it is used only as a mediator, not as IV or DV.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional analysis of 9,475 ABCD Study participants (N=11,878 at baseline, mean age 9.9 years); parents reported smartphone ownership age at baseline and annual follow-ups

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and first smartphone ownership age (binary outcome: <10 years vs ≥10 years)

FT_IC2.evidence: Phone-specific IV (age of first smartphone acquisition, dichotomized as <10 vs ≥10 years) paired exclusively with demographic-only outcomes (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education). Per criteria §2.4: 'Demographic-only (age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education, household composition, marital status, immigration status, urbanicity, study site) on the other side → NOT_MET regardless of whether the SM/phone variable itself qualifies.' No individual-trait-level outcomes (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, etc.) are analyzed; analyses examine only sociodemographic disparities in smartphone ownership timing. Table 2 logistic regression reports ORs for female (1.38), Black race (2.78), Hispanic (1.36), multiracial (1.69), single parent (2.01), living with partner (1.90), parental education <bachelor's (1.70) — all demographic/socioeconomic predictors, no psychological or health outcomes.

notes: Descriptive and inferential study of sociodemographic disparities in early smartphone ownership timing. No association tested between phone use and individual-trait outcomes; focus is purely on demographic predictors of ownership age.
